text: Living at moderate altitude (2,500 m) while training at low altitude (1,250 m) for 4 weeks improves 5,000-m time trial performance by approximately 13.4 seconds in competitive runners.
Both live-high/train-high and live-high/train-low protocols increase VO2max by approximately 5% through a 9% increase in red cell mass volume.
specific_value: 5% VO2max increase, 9% red cell mass increase

text: Living and training at altitude (high-high) increases VO2max and red cell mass but does not improve sea-level 5,000-m race performance, likely due to reduced training velocities at altitude.
The performance benefit of live-high/train-low over live-high/train-high is attributed to maintaining sea-level training velocities, which improves velocity at VO2max and maximal steady state.
The improvement in 5,000-m time from live-high/train-low is directly proportional to the increase in VO2max (r = 0.65).
An optimal live-high/train-low protocol uses a living altitude of approximately 2,500 m and a training altitude of approximately 1,250 m for a minimum of 4 weeks.
The increase in red cell mass volume from altitude acclimatization is correlated with the increase in VO2max (r = 0.37).
